entry.title: Stuffed (again) - 26 to 29 May
entry.content:
3-day week for me, felt like 6.

What was good

Lots, as we finished up May’s sprint.

We successfully published an update to the framework, including updating the user-centred design skills and adding them to the content designer role description.

This marks a few important things:

  1. All of the 50+ role descriptions in the framework have now been updated in some small or large way in the last 2 years.
  2. We’re still living up to our goal of community-driven, continuous improvement, iterating content based on our users’ experience and government’s needs.
  3. This time, I have directly shaped the career development of my own discipline - which is both exciting and nerve-wracking.

We’re getting an official blog post published soon that goes into more detail about this recent work, so I’ll leave it there for now. But I will need to take some time to reflect properly on it all.

This week, I also started thinking and talking about more robust, and public, ways to explaining what’s going on ‘behind the scenes’ of the framework’s content design. There’s a few factors coalescing that make it valuable to try something new.

  1. We spend a lot of effort keeping stakeholders and the community aware of what’s going on:
    • what work is in progress, and latest updates
    • who is involved, and how to get involved
    • explaining what’s changing and why
    • giving access to the work itself
  2. We’re moving off Google products imminently so a previous dashboard that did some this but used Data Studio has stopped being updated - and the wider point is that we should be aiming for something more timeless.
  3. We’re looking at moving everything to one of our two CMSes, the one with a simpler publishing workflow and better user management.
  4. There’s a refreshed commitment to working in the open around the place, so I think the leadership support is there.

In my head it’s something like the Planning Data project pages for each bit of work, mixed with occasional posts similar to GOV.UK AI Studio.

Other wins this week:

  • managed two afternoons of (nearly) uninterrupted focus time in a 3-day week
  • we squeezed in planning a new governance model for the other profession, next sprint we’ll make it happen
  • our product manager ran a workshop on defining service outcomes, bringing in adjacent teams that depend on our products
  • monthly governance processes like voting and meetings seem to be rolling nicely, despite high volume of proposals

What was challenging

Getting through the publishing process in good time, in a short week, where we also had our monthly governance meeting.

This was the first update for a long while without my wonderful contractor colleague who’d been able to focus on designing and publishing updates. For various reasons, I do not currently have that kind of focus available to me.

All the publishing work is currently on me, apart from some 2i support. Ideally I’d have got my new-ish colleague more involved in the preparation, but it’s all felt rushed since I came back from leave. I should try for for the next update.

Looking at the next month, I’ve set a lot plates spinning as part of the next update. In theory, they are mostly simple plates. In practice, it might not take much unexpected stuff to knock them down. And as we know the only thing you can expect is the unexpected.

What’s next

A 2-day week, due to family wedding up the the Lakes.

Other things

I voted online for the French consular elections. Always interesting interesting to experience the services of our neighbours.

You can very clearly see the joins between the parts of the overall journey (wordy, hard to use emails from consulate vs simple, clear central voting platform). But the fact that I can actually vote online in French elections is objectively great.

Finished two books!

1: The Regency Switch by Helen Gaskell - Very witty, very romancey. Really enjoyed reading something completely different to my usual genres. I used to work with Helen, so the best bit was hearing her voice come through so strong, it was almost like an imaginary audio book. And Helen has even reviewed it herself.

2: Why Design is Hard by Scott Berkun and Bryan Zug - Home truths, and practical advice, for any designer struggling with getting good work done in complex organisations. Chimed with lots of what I’ve learned over the last few years, so it’s great to have it all packaged up in portable paperback format to reflect back on/hold up to the camera in design community calls.

Reading and listening

Working in the open ≠ comms - Giles Turnbull

Glue work - Tanya Reilly

7 things you may not know about web accessibility - Andrew Tipp

Using agentic AI to curate tasks for users - Pete Kowalczyk

When orgs decide without thinking, UX bears the cost - Pavel Samsonov

Context of use - Bad Services podcast

entry.title: Document - 18 to 22 May
entry.content:
What was good

A lot of design work - if design includes documenting and communicating, which I think it does.

I’ve been preparing us for the next content update to the framework at the end of the month. As usual, we share the upcoming changes with the capability leads across government in advance so they can prepare to change their own systems, if they want to keep aligned. Received some nice feedback on how valuable this was, and some ideas for improving what’s communicated next time. Content design gold, I tell ya.

I’m also trying something new. As we’re fully updating a role, I’ve been looking at how we can do more to help content designers understand the changes, which seem quite big on the surface.

The lead of the working group has drafted a great blog post explaining the work, and I’ve signed up to support heads of content with making some materials to help people re-map their existing evidence to the new skills. So I’ve been sketching simple tables and visuals to show how the new skill descriptions relate to the old ones. I’m also considering how we can help people access the previous skill descriptions if they need them, for example using the UK Web Government Archive version.

Also this week, and very timely for Digital Accessibility Week, we received the results from the independent accessibility audit of the two websites we’re responsible for. As expected each one has a couple of small issues, so I’m hopeful these can be fixed ASAP. Overall, though, the results were really positive (better than expected?), with several shoutouts for good practice so am very proud of the team’s efforts, past and present.

The report also has includes helpful ‘observations’ to improve user experience. As an example of how thorough they were, although we had praise for our well structured CSVs which include text in cells with no data, they suggested even more descriptive text we could use. That’s my kind of attention to detail.

I usually try to keep Friday mornings clear for ‘documentation and community stuff’. This Friday, I somehow managed to make that most of the day, which was really satisfying. In particular, I spent the afternoon identifying and writing up the rationale for changes to the design skills. I’ll then share this with the heads of design group, again to help their designers understand what has changed and why. A huge thank you to my previous self for keeping every single revision and decision since October in a single document.

On this theme, I successfully migrated our ‘content inventory’ data to Jira. The inventory contains the metadata and design history for the 300+ content objects we look after (things like individual role and skill descriptions, but also guidance content and even forms we use as part of our governance service). I’m sure my future self will be thanking me again for this before too long.

Finally, I was able to confirm I’ll be doing a session at Service Design in Government in September. The session will be on what we’ve learned from community-led governance and co-design for the framework over the last few years. Going to SDinGov was a big highlight from last year, so I am Very Excited and also Quite Nervous to actually be speaking there.

What was challenging

The team is still quite confused when it comes to defining and planning work in our new Jira world, and in the meetings about doing those things. I’m having to resist jumping in too much and making it more confusing and need to trust others to own their roles. I’m a) hopeful that from next sprint we will start to get in the groove and b) worried that a much larger ‘migration’ that’s coming our way is going to derail us.

I’ve once again failed to properly recognise and prepare for an upcoming super short week. But at least I’ve spotted the one immediately after that, promise.

Reading

The logic of conjecture, part 2: Wicked problems at systems scale - Mike Gallagher

How digital prevention services fit with neighbourhood health - NHS design histories

Re-shaping (public sector) user centred design for an AI assisted world - Leisa Reichelt

The largest review of synthetic participants ever conducted found exactly what you’d expect. Synthetic users don’t work - The Voice of User

Why all content is fundamentally words - Andrew Tipp

Navigating the unpredictability of everything - Jason Cohen

entry.title: Octopus - 11 to 15 May
entry.content:
Work

Interesting week with lots of work with groups across government.

First thing Tuesday I met with the lead of the group working on an update for the data engineer role. We checked feedback from the community fact check process - this process gives us confidence whether proposed changes to a skill description will work for different roles and in different organisational contexts. I liked doing this kind of work first thing, really set me up for the rest of the day.

Then Thursday I met with both a group of business analysts and a group of content designers. This was the first time I’d properly met with the business analysts as a former colleague had been facilitating this work. They seem a really good bunch with a clear sense of what they want to achieve so I’m looking forward to helping them finish off their updates over the summer.

The content designer group are at the other end of the process. We agreed final edits in response to highlighter testing in April, and the role description will now be updated at the end of the month.

The changes to the content designer role will mean that all 50+ role descriptions in this capability framework have been updated in some way, large or small, in the last 2 years. The team needs to find a way to celebrate this and share wider within the organisation. I may even have to do a cringe Linkedin post.

I’m continuing to support my new-ish content designer colleague in sessions with stakeholders when I can. They’re definitely now taking the lead on the content in their area, which is great to see. We’re enjoying showing the people we talk to our ‘big map of everything related to a profession capability framework’.

The map uses a narrative object modelling approach and is very scrappy by design. It’s hopefully helping everyone recognise the complexity of the area and align on shared language.

This week we’ve had early results from the independent accessibility audit of both websites our team has built and maintained. The results are mostly really positive and the auditors have commented on how many good practices they’ve seen. It’s really satisfying to have external recognition of our work in this area, especially as our sites adapt standard design system components in new ways.

More Jira wangling over the week. One success has been including a hypothesis template in our default epic/story card, though I’ve called it an ‘idea definition statement’. It sort of looks like this:

Because of (evidence of problem),
We think that changing (existing thing) to (new state)
Will lead to (improvement for our users)
We will know this works if we see (measurable change).

The thinking is that we can use this with stakeholders to refine ideas, while moving to using design hypotheses as the basis for much of the team’s work.

Finally, there has been rumbles of starting up a UCD community of practice for our directorate so I was asked how the regular drop-in for content designers I run is going.

I’ve reflected that the value in it comes from its small size (usually 3-5 people) and focus on content-related stuff. This means people are comfortable sharing work in progress and getting feedback on problems we have. It also makes it easier to build and maintain relationships, which comes in handy when our work overlaps so often.

Other things

The next X-Gov Sheffield breakfast meetup is on 2 June! Sadly I’m away for this one, but don’t let that stop you signing up if you’re in the area.

I’ve been savouring an early birthday present for myself, a big heavy book about the legendary designer, Margaret Calvert, Woman At Work.

Lovely book to have lying around and Kid A has enjoyed flicking through all the pictures of Calvert’s designs for lettering and signs. We even got inspired enough to draw our own signs. This one says ‘Warning: Octopuses’:

Reading

Output isn’t design - Karri Saarinen

The logic of conjecture: part 1 - Mike Gallagher

Service design is not user experience design at greater scale - Simon Morgan-Wilson

The appointments problem: why it’s harder than it looks - NHS design histories

Lobster pots, complexity and starting small in the right way - Richard Pope

100 days is enough to prove a different way of governing - Transform Wales

AI, open code and vulnerability risk in the public sector - GDS guidance

What this page knows since you arrived

entry.title: Stuffed - 5 to 8 May
entry.content:
Work

Classic 3 day week for me, stuffed to the brim. Need to spot these much further in advance so I can set more realistic expectations for myself.

Nevertheless, valuable stuff achieved.

It was the first week of our ‘publishing sprint’ which goes to the end of the month. This meant sprint planning on Tuesday, and this meant wangling around with Jira, which the directorate switched to behind my back while I was on leave last month. Rude.

We made good progress configuring boards and ‘issues’ and other things so they are useful (i.e. removing nearly everything in sight), and even planned some work. 7/10, would epic again.

(Sidenote: this week I got to write a justification for migrating some data to Jira that included “(the data) contains the design history and metadata, going back 10 years, for 350 content objects our team maintains to enable recruitment for 2 government professions”. Funny how these things build up. Also, please don’t delete it.)

Last week the team lost our wonderful developer, Daniella, to another team. This week we gained a new user researcher, who seems ace. Next week we need to find someone to help with development, probably from another team. This is either the ‘circle of life’ or our version of dual track development.

Also on Tuesday I got enthusiastic approval from our deputy director to do something exciting. Now just need organisation approval… Watch this space.

I broke my ‘no-work Wednesday’ rule to drop in on the working group updating the skills for ‘content designer’ as they discussed which levels of the role required which proficiency of the skills. I then spent Thursday and Friday assessing the collated results from highlighter testing and making edits in response. We are finalising content next week. We are very nearly there.

As it turned out, Thursday PM was my Q4/end of year review. Good honest discussion was had, where I was maybe too honest in places, probably due to tiredness. Luckily lots of lovely things were said which made me very embarrassed. Here’s to a great ‘performance year 2026 to 2027’.

Finally on Friday, I got to hang out with a couple of other GDS content designers in a government office in actual Sheffield. This was great, let’s do it more. There are literally half-half-dozens of us!

Other things

Updated my Now page for May, including a way to show previous ‘nows’.

Continued making small ‘quality of experience’ updates to the old Rising Ape Collective website, simplifying the menu and removing out of date ‘about’ information about people. Quite zen, feels like digital gardening.

I seem to have started a small book buying habit (7 over the last month) that runs directly contrary to my book reading habit (maybe 2 in a year). I’m hoping the sheer pressure of those unread words forces me to find the time and pick up the pace.

Reading

How to do a website content audit - Lauren Pope

The “Opposite Test” - Jason Cohen

The IKEA effect: When labour leads to love (PDF) - Michael I. Norton, Daniel Mochon, Dan Ariely

Progressive data enhancement - James Darling

There is no such thing as the government - Stefan Czerniawski

A dozen interns on cocaine: What running one the longest-running civic tech project reveals about AI in government - Beth Simone Noveck

On maintenance - Sarah Fisher

entry.title: Leave - April 2026
entry.content:
I was on paternity leave for most of April, which has been magical. And while I was supposed to be in full on baby mode, my brain couldn’t help doing other things. Here are some of those things…

Tinkering with this site

Most mornings I’ve been able to take Kid B out to a cafe for an hour or two. Aside from reading, sometimes I messed around with this website.

Things I added:

  • Links page, to collect links from weeknotes
  • Now page, to explain what I’m currently focused on
  • Contact page, so people can reach out
  • RSS feed, like all the cool kids have
  • a site footer menu, to include links to some of the added things

Things I thought about:

  • changing font family and background colour
  • documenting tools I like to use, like Obsidian and Twine (see below)
  • making more of tags
  • writing things that aren’t only weeknotes

Finding useful things

Found and tested out a couple of tools that could prove useful for the day job.

Mist - created by Matt Webb, Mist allows multiple people to work on the same markdown file, includes comments.

Helpful for: avoiding trying to “collaborate” in Word.

Twine - primarily designed for creating ‘choose your own adventure’-style interactive fiction, Twine also work as a basic HTML prototype generator. You can copy in pages of markdown to quickly create a set of linked pages with very little technical knowledge. And because it’s just a file, a user can open it easily in their browser for testing.

We actually used Twine to sketch out our interactive theatre shows for Rising Ape (see below) and I am made up to remember it and work out how useful it is for content design. I should probably write this up into its own post.

Helpful for: avoiding faffing in Figma and risking inaccessible prototypes

NetNewsWire + Feedly - Yes, RSS feed readers. Rest assured, if you’re writing interesting stuff, I am typing ‘/feed’ in your sites URL and grabbing it.

Helpful for: avoiding “social” media.

Sorting out an old website

I caught up with my pal James Riley and we talked about what to do with the website for our old side hustle/creative outlet, Rising Ape Collective.

We decided we’re going to keep the site up, and make clear that the group is now longer active. The running costs are very low and we think it’s still a useful record of what the people in Rising Ape achieved in the fields of science communication and public engagement with research. The live events in particular were so much fun to create, and you never know what ideas might be sparked by having them documented.

I contacted everyone we worked with to see if they were ok with keeping their name and role on the site. Obviously, it’s important that people can control the info about themselves on the internet. It’s also been lovely to hear back from everyone and see what they’re up to.

There’s also been some cool comments on how getting involved with Rising Ape helped people’s careers. We’d always thought of the group as a kind of ‘scicomm accelerator’ that gave people space to experiment and get experience in different parts of the field. It’s super nice to hear this validated years later. And maybe one day, the Ape will rise again?

Reading books

Incredibly, I read, from cover to cover, 3 (THREE) books in April.

The Great Zoo of China by Matthew Reilly - A handy book to start while nervously waiting in a hospital. Absolutely ridiculous.

Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde, and other short stories by Robert Louis Stevenson - So victorian it hurt.

The Beach by Alex Garland - Made me miss travelling. Felt very ‘first book’. Nicely unhinged.

High on my success, I just ordered 5 more books including The Regency Switch by brilliant former colleague Helen Gaskell. Stay tuned for a review in, oh I don’t know, 3 years?

Reading the internet

I also read a lot of great blogs and articles. Way too many to link here, so here is small selection and the rest have been saved on the Links page.

  • AI Prototyping: Harder. Worser. Faster. Wronger. - Anna E Cook
  • What being self-employed-content-really-looks-like - Lauren Pope
  • Because coordination is expensive - Lorin Hochstein
  • One design system is better than 2 - Frankie Roberto
  • From Garbage Can to Compost Heap - Mike Gallagher

Working a bit

The last week of April I settled back into work after 4 weeks off. We did some quite intense working on ‘ways of working’. Quite tough for a first week back but necessary given our new team members and a migration to Jira while I was away. I also squeaked in some design work, collating and analysing responses from a highlighter test on the updated skill descriptions for designers.

Next week is the start of our ‘publishing sprint’ to the end of the month. I’ll be working closely with our cross-government working groups to finalise changes to content for the Digital and Data Profession Capability Framework, to go live on 29 May. This should include the long awaited changes to the ‘content designer’ role, which is pleasingly meta.

entry.title: New - 23 to 27 March
entry.content:
Work week

Spent the week trying to get things in place as I expected to start 4 weeks of paternity leave at any moment from next week.

Mostly this meant:

  • a lot of discussions and planning of what would be possible in the next quarter with reduced team capacity (we also have vacant roles)
  • sharing useful existing documentation, like our team playbook and design hypotheses, with our new product manager
  • checking that new content designer knows where things are at with the framework content for the new profession so they can run with it from now on
  • helping new delivery manager run our monthly governance meeting and set up the voting process for two proposals using GOV.UK Forms and Notify
  • some of my main job - trying to do what I said I would do for each of the working groups currently drafting new descriptions, either:
    • scheduling co-writing sessions for when I’m back
    • editing draft content ready for wider community fact-checking in April

All these were accomplished to varying levels of success (in the order above, from ‘most sorted’ to ‘least sorted’) and none were completed. This is probably because I was attempting them all at once. So it goes.

A lot of my thinking was, “it’s ok, I can finish this, this and this on Monday”.

But there’s a lot of ‘new’ next to those roles in my team, so I was feeling the pressure to set people up for success in case I went off suddenly.

Well that Monday morning brought our new baby daughter, 10 days ‘early’.

And I think everything’s going to be fine.

See y’all in 4 weeks!

Reading

  • Navigating the labyrinth: Socio-economic background and career progression within the Civil Service
  • I built an AI service mapping tool - Sarah Drummond
  • Content architecture is not content infrastructure - Joe Phillips
  • Mist: Share and edit Markdown together, quickly - Matt Webb
  • When AI answers the question, what happens to the journey? - Mark Edwards
  • A new flatpack collaborative model for the public sector - Will Callaghan
  • Reality drift - Steve Messer
  • On failure. Or, one song to the tune of another - Sarah Fisher

entry.title: Human - 16 to 20 March
entry.content:
Work week

Great start to the week on Monday, thanks to the marvellous Giles Turnbull presenting to the organisation on the benefits of ‘writing like a human’, even internally. I’ve very briefly worked with Giles before in a past job (he absolutely won’t remember), and long followed his writing on all things communication, so it was particularly great to see leadership visibly endorsing his approaches. Hopefully this translates into more real, direct voices in our communication across the organisation. It’s certainly given me more confidence to 1) be more human in my writing both here and at work, and 2) make suggestions to colleagues on their writing where it’s helpful, even if it’s just “can we use the actual 3 word name for this thing, instead of 4 different acronyms?”.

On Tuesday I helped host the latest ‘X-Gov Sheffield’ breakfast and lunch meetups for public sector folk around here. Once again, the diversity of people who turned up is really cool: different councils and departments, designers, policy people, change management people, HR people, even Network Rail… I’m especially pleased to see that local government people from different organisations seem to have taken to the idea. Think they get a lot of value from both meeting other kinds of ‘local’ people, and mixing with the central government people. Myself and the co-organisers had a little retro later in the week, and brainstormed some nice ideas to boost the effects of this informal networking in future.

Day job work things this week are a bit blurry, so let’s see what I can remember. So much going on and changing across my team and wider… think it’s felt like a lot of helping others get clarity on a way forward, nudging towards open working, but not much time for my own stuff.

On Thursday, my fellow framework content designer and I joined a content strategy workshop run by another team looking at a whole domain of government guidance we’re a part of. The facilitators did a great job bringing together a wide range of stakeholders and getting different views out, but mainly due to tool access issues, it didn’t feel like we had the space for structured input and thinking. Good reminder for our team on getting the basics of collaboration in place. For example, what tools can we use that do not need accounts? Or could a simple document have been used instead of flashier virtual whiteboard?

Also on Thursday we said a sad goodbye to our passionate service owner. They’ve done a valiant job for a year, and I think there will be some really interesting long term positive effects from their ideas. Just wish making a difference didn’t often feel like a long slog for reformers/transformers in the public sector. More happily I’ve heard that interviews for their replacement have gone very well. Fingers crossed they can pick up the baton with a refreshed energy and focus.

Related, the team’s OKR approach from last week seems to have landed and is helping the new co-leaders of our area. They’re also taking a suggestion of some sort of leadership health metric seriously and are trying to put one or two in place.

This suggestion came about because I have a new annoying habit: whenever I hear someone say “we’re taking a test and learn approach with this”, but with no more details, I say “that’s great to hear. So how will you know if what we’re testing is working, and how can we spread what we learn?”.

Finally, on Friday I met with a couple of heads of delivery for a kick off. This group have had their proposal for a new role description in the ‘product and delivery’ role family of the Digital and Data Profession Capability Framework accepted by our governance council - so now the fun stuff!

We met so I could scope the kind of design work needed (how established is this role? How similar is it across government? How certain are they about those things?), and I could take them through our guidance and templates. This has reminded me that I need to set down in more detail my principles for designing effective role and skill descriptions. Maybe something for a future weeknote?

Other things

The second half of the week was shadowed over by a funky headcold. In turn I realised mid-Thursday my patience had become as thin as an elephant-arse-crushed sticky bun - only natural when ill and tired. But I don’t want to stay in this zone for too long, I need to live up to my mugs, after all.

Reading

  • What went wrong with the BOM redesign? - Leisa Reichelt
  • Escaping the product operating model - Scott Colfer
  • How the Netherlands bent bureaucracy into something beautiful - Michaela Haas
  • Leading AI product innovation on planning.data.gov.uk - Steve Messer
  • Was prototyping really a bottleneck?- Frank Elavsky
  • The “solution-first” survival playbook from GovCamp 2026 - Oli Lovell

entry.title: Vertigo - 9 to 13 March
entry.content:
Work week

This week has felt typically busy but there has been a thread of focus threatening to creep through. Looking back, there has been a lot of positive movement.

The best thing has been getting back to grips with the work to update content of the Digital and Data framework, both moving current things forward and scheduling work for later this year. In particular, I was able to get a draft of the updated design skills out into the wider community for testing.

This is obviously close to my heart. The aim of the work is to ensure the skill descriptions can be useful for content designers, positioning us clearly as designers, and apply some lessons learned after a year of use by other design roles.

So far, I’ve mainly been working with ‘subject matter experts’ like lead content designers and heads of design across government to check and re-draft the descriptions of the skills. But almost by definition, experts cannot judge how clearly they are communicating their domain to others. So now we’re looking to test the clarity of the descriptions with some typical users, in this case content, service, interaction and graphic designers of all levels.

After some ‘testing of testing’ at a content community day last month, we’ve settled on a version of a trusty highlighter test. The results should be coming in over the next month, which we’ll use to finalise the next iteration of the skill descriptions to be published end of May.

Elsewhere we have a more realistic delivery timeline for the new profession website. This is really helpful as it means we can ensure the site is robust from launch and get in place the supporting organisational infrastructure it will need long term. This week I’ve been involved in everything from the very high level ‘where is this thing going, and why?’ discussions, to the nitty gritty of translating ‘profession brand’ into decisions and principles for customising design system components.

Stepping back, this amount of zooming in and out in one week is a little vertigo inducing. But, I dunno, I kind of enjoy it? Everything is connected, the big things affect the little things and vice versa, and I think it’s part of a designer’s job to do this connecting, translating, sense-making across different scales. Somebody has to produce coherence for both the organisation and the user’s experience… I can’t see anyone else doing it, anyway.

Another nice thing: Both the team’s community-led governance process and our (relatively) open ways of working got a big shout out at the first show and tell for our new directorate. The fact this came from another, high profile, team was particularly heart-warming. It’s nice to know what we do is noticed and has impact on how our colleagues might decide to go about their work.

Finally, we even found time for two sessions drafting team OKRs for next quarter. This isn’t (yet) an established process in our part of the organisation but we know it’s critical for us to have clear priorities, given how our core products act as common components enabling such a wide range of services and processes. I facilitated the sessions so might be biased, but feel we came out with a refreshed sense of purpose and direction. The team knows what needs doing over this next period, what’s valuable. Hopefully we can get the objectives agreed with leadership next week, and then get on with it.

Finally finally, and related to the OKRs, we squeezed in a couple of speculative discussions with other teams that hint at some exciting potential futures we could help make happen. Another dimension of enjoyable vertigo: zooming ahead, seeing a better future, then coming back to the present to make better decisions in the now.

Other things

No note last week as I spent my allotted ‘non-work’ work time over the weekend submitting a pitch for Service Design in Government. Quite fun to think about what others might find valuable about the team’s experience. Now, the waiting game…

Next Tuesday, I’m running the next X-Gov breakfast and lunch meetup for public sector people in Sheffield. We’ve had a fantastic, diverse, response once again, but still time to sign up if you’re around South Yorkshire!

Reading and watching

  • The web’s grain - Frank Chimero
  • From problem to solution without handover: the dual-track method - Germany’s Digital Service
  • How Crash Bandicoot hacked the original Playstation - Ars Technica
  • Lean governance for digital service delivery - Rumman Amin
  • Service leadership - Kate Tarling
  • Why content design exists - Rich Prowse

entry.title: Requirement - 23 to 27 February
entry.content:
Work week

Started the week confirming we were delaying the site launch originally planned for Thursday. Instead we will make use of support from some very helpful developers from another team to build something easier to deploy and maintain longer term.

Cue a slightly awkward call on Tuesday where new team asks us for the list of requirements our developer has been working to. We’ve just been moving too fast, and re-using our existing knowledge, tech and decisions, to formally specify things in a way that a brand new developer could use. We have everything they need, it’s just a little… separated.

Handily the original site in development is so close to what we need for launch that it works as one big requirement. Even more luckily, it turns out that the other team were already exploring using Wagtail CMS for their own products. They just needed something to really test it out on…

So we’ve quickly got a rough new version of the site up and running, and the CMS looks to do what we need it to in terms of of structured content. Despite the delay of a couple weeks I’m hopeful we’ll be in a much better place to deliver value for users and the wider team longer term. I’m also heartened that we’re building a relationship with a like-minded team that could lead to smarter working in future.

I’ve realised we need to make sure we’re communicating this in the story of the work: we’ve essentially built and tested a very high fidelity prototype over 8 days, rapidly working out what was feasible and what was risky. Sharing this learning openly and widely has then allowed us to build a better product closer to what both users and the organisation needs.

I then spent most of the rest of the week quickly gathering together existing documentation and ideas, like our content models and frontend design decisions, into accessible, coherent requirements. This has been quietly satisfying work. By Thursday I was even starting to feel like a designer again.

Also on Thursday we held our monthly cross-government ‘design council’ for the Digital and Data profession’s framework. There were no proposals for representatives to review this month, so we took the chance to explain the above work, and share a survey to help us understand how organisations go about making decisions about proposals. We also let everyone know that due to the reduced team capacity for the next 2 months, we are re-planning all the content development work currently in progress.

I’m still pushing to get the skills update for the ‘content designer’ role published in May. This extremely meta work (designing content for content designers to assess their design skills) now just needs test materials for the latest draft for heads of content/design to share with a sample of designers in their organisations. Finding the time to create said test material is another thing.

At the end of the week my line manager passed on some nice feedback from leadership on my approach to the challenges this past few weeks. Have to say, it really does make a difference to personal morale. I need to make sure there’s public recognition of the team’s work soon.

Other things

Started working on my pitch for SDinGov. The theme this year is ‘democratising design’.

I’ve enjoyed considering what people might take from our experience running a product where key users can propose significant changes - and then have cross-government representatives actually vote on them.

I’m thinking about where this level of participation might be useful in other contexts, but also where it might cause issues! Deadline to submit is 9 March.

Reading

  • From outputs to outcomes: redefining public sector success - Transform Wales
  • A New Communities of Practice Model for Organisational Maturity - Emily Webber
  • Transforming health and delivering the NHS 10-year plan - Richard Pope
  • Documenting a service as a content designer - Amy Noss
  • What Do Companies Actually Want from Content Designers in 2026? - Ademola Adepoju

entry.title: Realistic - 16 to 20 February
entry.content:
Work week

Bit of a reality check, after thinking about fun at work last week.

Over this week our small but mighty team has had blockers and unexpected events pop up, each one potentially derailing us ahead of our launch date. Cumulatively this reached a point where on Thursday morning we made a tough call on what can be realistically delivered next week.

We’ve been offered support for the team, so there’s still a chance to meet the original scope. Even so I hope we’ve learned:

  • everything will always take longer than expected, and things will always go wrong - so build in that expectation, avoid 100% guarantees to other stakeholders, always have an acceptable plan B agreed
  • we can be more confident in explaining right from the start how precarious technical delivery is going to be with a short timeframe and a small team

On the plus side it has been great to see the core team pull together, especially the newer members. We’re bringing our own strengths, supporting each other, working things through. It’s definitely been a bonding experience, which should help with future challenges.

Myself, as we’re temporarily short of experienced management roles (who know how to navigate the organisation) I’ve been stepping in to support every other bit of delivery, and almost park my actual design role. I’ve had to reckon with how the resulting context switching and extended responsibility has affected both my mood in and out of work, and my physical ability to actually do work. There was a point on Thursday where I felt totally jammed, which hasn’t happened for a long, long time. So yes, it’s all experience for the future CV/interviews, but also something to think carefully about if this becomes a regular situation.

Weirdly, a job ad in another department popped up this week which is almost a carbon copy of this temporary super-hybrid role I’ve ended up in the last 2 weeks (delivery management-service-designer-plus-content-and frontend-design-and-ops-thereof-oh-and-also-product management). All I can say, from my brief experience, is “errr I’m totally not sure it’s a realistic, sustainable, full time role for one person, maybe…?”.

Amongst this have managed a couple of good chats with like-minded people in adjacent and not so adjacent parts of the organisation, helping with perspective and sparking ideas. It’s a good place because of good people, ultimately.

Other things

Set up the next meetup day for public sector folk in Sheffield on Tuesday 17 March, loads of interest already - if you’re round the South Yorkshire area, join us! Find out more and sign up for X-Gov Sheffield breakfast/lunch.

Attended a helpful design community session about pitching for Service Design in Government. No excuses now.

Played tag rugby on Tuesday evening in -2C, and really struggled physically over the next couple of days. So maybe I’m just ill 🤷.

Reading

  • Tackling technical debt without stopping delivery
  • Britain Lost 14,000 Third Places. They Were Called Pubs. Is Your Local Next?
  • AI and the end of friction as a policy lever
  • A decade later – launching the ONS website in 2016
  • How to design a style guide (that people can actually use)
  • The public deserves services that are useful, usable and safe

entry.title: Hack - 9 to 13 February
entry.content:
Work week

The week started with the decision on platform choice we’ve been treading water waiting for. Happily it’s ended up nearer to the third way I’d advocated last week: Launching a stripped down ‘beta’ site using our established tech and frontend, while setting expectations that more functionality and content will be coming through a public roadmap.

But then of course the real work starts, and with a gnarly hard deadline coming up fast before the end of the month. So lots of getting the team together, setting clear goals each day, working out what really needs doing and where we need others onside. We’re also working radically in the open (compared to our wider team) using the justification that it will save time all round.

It was typically hard crunchy work for first 2 days. First to define, agree and communicate our scope, then some rapid design and tech decision making, but amazingly we were already set to demo a prototype site to a group of cross-government stakeholder/users by Wednesday afternoon. I wasn’t there (still non-working/parenting day Wednesdays for me, going strong since 2022💪 ) but judging by feedback, the team, including brand new content designer, seems to have smashed the session out of the park.

Building and maintaining trust with users in departments is maybe the most important part of our work. Our approach has been to carefully explain not just what we’re confident in, but also where we’re less certain, especially about future plans. We’re also constantly asking for input and feedback to learn about the realities of departments we’re looking to support. It’s really heartening to see this honest approach get buy in from our leaders and those outside the organisation.

The end of my week did get consumed by too much domain wrangling and testing set up, and not enough design, but I’m still confident that something useful will be in place by deadline.

On a less positive note, our preferred candidate for the user researcher role to support all this work long term has dropped out. Not quite unexpected, but we may need to get creative to fill this position sooner than the 5 months it took to not get someone in at all.

Hack-a-thon

Amongst the above, I was somehow able to keep a 2.5 hour block in my calendar free to take part in the latest Design System Hackathon run by GDS, and the Ministry of Justice team running the new GOV Reuse Library.

I’m very glad I blocked out the time. The facilitators did a great job setting a playful tone and leading a group of 100+ through a fully online ‘hack’ session.

Our goal was to ‘inspire’ a team starting to look at improving how designers and devs building services can discover and use components and patterns in the ecosystem of public sector design systems that now exist. For example:

  • GOV.UK Design System
  • Ministry of Justice Design System
  • Scottish Government Design System
  • NHS Design System
  • Office for National Statistics Design System
  • Department for Work and Pensions Design System

My lovely group had a lot of fun fleshing out our idea: ‘The Great Design System Bakeoff’, a 3 day challenge where teams of cross gov digital people compete to build the most chimeric public sector service possible, with the winners voted for by the wider community. Our proposition was this would:

  • take advantage of the current situation where learning about design systems is often reliant on relationships and connections
  • build relationships and community across organisations (and therefore shared understanding of different systems)
  • leverage the power of “razzmatazz” to highlight the various systems and the valuable work of UCD teams

No idea if this will prove useful to the facilitators (most ideas were a little more grounded and had less photoshopping of Noel and Prue). But since the GDS Design community session last week I’ve been thinking a lot about ‘having fun’ at work, and the serious benefits that can result. This felt a perfect opportunity to give that a go, so am excited to see what happens next.

To do

  • Set up the next ‘X-Gov Sheffield meetup’ for Tuesday 3 March
  • Enjoy the Winter Olympics while we have it. It’s ridiculous and great, isn’t it.

Reading

  • Shifting Roles - From Content Professionals to Ontologists
  • Leadership health metrics
  • Sheffield Design Lab
  • Seeing Everything, Understanding Nothing (The Context Trap)
  • Practice notes on including citizens in the design process

entry.title: Narrative - 2 to 6 February
entry.content:
One of those weeks where so much happens you almost can’t remember any of it. But then you sit down to write about it.

Monday: Started the day learning that a key technical decision underpinning the work we had planned for this quarter was being re-considered, and providing my thoughts to leaders. I can see valid arguments both ways, one side aiming to maintain a more coherent experience for users, the other looking to show something real has changed for the profession.

As ever I’m thinking of the third way, avoiding the big bang launch, and supporting users to gradually change their behaviour, moving content and functionality from one platform to another part by part, ideally testing as we go… Floated this around the unit, but ultimately it’s not going to be my call (or at least I don’t have the time to get more involved) and I have to be ok with that. Just need to help the team clearly communicate what can be delivered depending on the final choice, and the point that it’s made.

Tuesday: down to London and back in one day for a long workshop with colleagues across our unit on the future of the same work held up by the above decision. It was great to build some relationships in person and show the value of our team to every one else’s stuff. Most of this stuff is effectively services provided to departments to build their digital ‘capability’ (people). We should be able to sit down properly soon with each team to work out how to better support these services and who needs our help most. Also great to meet the ace new content designer in our team in person!

A nice surprise was the journey. Getting a train from Sheffield at 8:30, then back from St Pancras at 15:30, meant I can help with the morning routine and get home at a decent time for tea and bedtime (and then pub quiz). Would happily do this again if meeting times allow, so kudos to my manager for the considerate scheduling.

Thursday and Friday: Wins: managed one co-writing session each day with subject matter experts! Less positive: In the first, we learned that one of the main assumptions we were confident in is not right for all the role descriptions, just some of them. But that’s how it goes, by doing the work, we learn what the work is. Second session did make us more confident on that particular role, so overall it’s a draw on the role design front for this week.

Another thing these sessions reminded me is that no one properly understands the bigger picture of how different standards and frameworks related to our new one are all supposed to relate. So myself and new content designer spent a couple of hours using narrative object modelling to map out everything in the domain around the framework. Then, following my own frequent advice to others, shared this work in progress with others in the wider unit and even the cross-government structured information community (gulp). Happily this sparked lots of idea and appreciation in both places, which feels nice. Hopefully it can encourage others to share their work more openly too.

Amongst near endless onboarding these last couple of weeks, and a fair bit of shadowing, was able to hand over real design work to the new designer, some information architecture scoping, some content 2i. Very pleased to see nothing has phased them so far. They ask good questions, and I think they’re into the heavily strategic profile of the work. Early, but feels like all the effort to recruit well to the role has paid off 🤞.

Finally I managed to catch up on some plans for the organisation’s design community which are really exciting. I’m keen to make sure all types of designer, in all areas, are being thought about and feel the benefit, but overall it feels like having a Chief Designer in place has helped kicked things up a gear.

To do

  • Propose a session for SDinGov - going along last year was a big highlight, the theme of ‘democracy’ is perfect for our work, and I resolved to get out there more this year, so no excuses whatsoever. Deadline Monday 9 March.
  • Keep building out this site, maybe write out some case studies.

Reading

  • GOV.UK Prototype Kit - Data driven prototyping
  • Accessible design is digital infrastructure
  • Your brain does not process information and is not a computer
  • AI Skills Hub: a content critique
